data.title: Bain Capital Specialty Finance: P/NAV Of 0.78x, Double-Digit Yield; This BDC Is A Hidden Gem (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

As someone whose primary investment objective is income, the BDC (BIZD) sector is one I watch closely. I sold out of all my BDC holdings earlier this year due to anticipated headwinds from lower base rates and increased economic volatility. But many are much more attractive now that base rates have come down.

With more rate cuts anticipated in the coming months, investors expect more volatility. And while the sector has experienced a pullback over the past 10 months, many BDCs could be worth dollar-cost averaging into at current levels.

A BDC that currently fits the bill is Bain Capital Specialty Finance (NYSE:BCSF). And as a result of their discount to NAV and strong fundamentals, BCSF is one of the few BDCs I think could be worth nibbling on at the current price.

In this article I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Bain Capital Specialty Finance in January of this year, assigning the BDC a buy rating due to their strong credit quality, impressive earnings, and discount to its net asset value.

As one of the few smaller-sized BDCs I considered to be of high quality, BCSF's discount to its peers was compelling for income-focused investors. Despite this, their smaller size, increased economic volatility, and lower interest rates posed risks, something I cautioned investors about going forward.

Since, BCSF has seen its share price slide, down nearly 21% in the past nine months, while the S&P (SP500) has continued its strong performance, up 12.23%.

Solid Credit Quality Amidst Headwinds

Since my last coverage on BCSF, the BDC has reported earnings three times, their last earnings report this past August. As a result, mounting headwinds have been evident from lower base rates, as well as a tough economic backdrop.

The Fed has cut interest rates by 25 basis points, which has impacted the sector due to their predominantly floating-rate portfolios. However, heightened economic volatility from tariffs has also impacted the sector, leading to lower investment activity and deal volumes as well as tighter spreads.

However, BCSF has navigated well, as gross originations during the quarter actually increased from the prior quarter and year-over-year. These amounted to $530 million, up from $277 million in Q1. From the prior year's quarter, these rose an impressive 73%.

Of these, most were weighted toward first-lien loans at 93%, bringing the BDC's total to 63%. Net investment income continued on a sequential decline, amounting to $0.47 per share. This fell from $0.50 in Q1 and from $0.51 the previous year's quarter.

Total investment income was $71 million, a modest decline of $1.3 million year-over-year. This did manage to increase from the previous quarter's $66.84 million, a result of higher investment activity and higher yields.

But through this, BCSF's credit quality remained solid, with most of their borrowers rated 2.

Areas Of Concern

As a result of lower base rates and increased economic volatility, Bain Capital Specialty Finance's decline in their top and bottom lines wasn't a surprise. Especially considering the BDC has 93% exposure to floating-rate debt. But when assessing their financials, their bottom line declined on a total basis, something I was slightly concerned about.

I usually look for BDCs to grow their net investment income on a total basis, even if this declines on a per share basis. For example, Capital Southwest (CSWC), despite their NII declining from $0.64 to $0.61 year-over-year, actually grew slightly from $31.1 million to $31.2 million.

BCSF's NII declined from $33.1 million to $30.6 million. This also declined from Q1's $32.1 million. And this was the result of the BDC realizing a higher number of losses during the quarter.

Net realized and unrealized losses were $6.9 million, up from $3.6 million in the prior quarter and from $4 million in Q2 2024. BCSF added one name to non-accruals status, bringing their total non-performing loans to 1.7% at cost and 0.6% at fair value.

While this is a modest increase from last year's 1.2% at cost, this could be a signal that the economy is weakening. If so, this could lead to higher losses and lower income in the coming quarters. Coupled with lower interest rates, BCSF could see further downside.

But their fundamentals remain solid, and management could offset lower interest rates if deal volumes pick up in the coming months.

Something else that caused a bit of concern was that leverage increased significantly from the previous year. This was 1.22x on a pro forma basis. As a result, this was higher than both the peer average and brought leverage closer to the high end of management's targeted range of 1.0x–1.25x, which management attributed to higher cash levels.

This also increased from 1.03x in the previous year's quarter. But besides that, their balance sheet remained solid with enough available liquidity to satisfy their upcoming debt maturities in March & October of 2026 at $796 million.

Despite their liquidity and investment-grade credit ratings, it's likely the BDC will have to refinance their 2026 debt at higher rates, another headwind that could drag on their bottom line. Both their March & October debt have weighted-average interest rates of less than 3%.

NAV & Dividend Sustainability

What should have been expected due to the higher increase in net realized and unrealized losses, BCSF's NAV was negatively impacted. NAV declined $0.08 from the prior quarter and $0.14 from the prior year.

Regarding their dividend sustainability, the BDC still managed to cover its dividend payout of $0.45 with dividend coverage of 104%. This included their base dividend of $0.42 and supplemental dividend of $0.03. But I do believe the company can sustain their dividend near term as they had ample spillover of $1.43, which provides a temporary buffer.

Additionally, management stated during Q2 that they had an additional $0.10 in undistributed taxable income from joint venture investments. Going forward, I expect management to eliminate the supplemental dividend if net investment income continues to decline while maintaining their $0.42 base dividend.

Attractive Valuation

Although I don't consider Bain Capital Specialty Finance to be in the same tier as peers like Ares Capital (ARCC) or Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), buying any BDC at a discount below NAV is attractive. Especially if you're an income-focused investor.

At the current price, BCSF has a P/NAV ratio of 0.78x. This is slightly higher than their three-year average discount of 13.77%. The 19.5% discount is also close to their one-year low of 21.94%, which some investors may find attractive. However, I'd suggest waiting for more clarity on additional rate cuts, likely by the end of the year.

Risks & Conclusion

For many BDCs, especially smaller ones like BCSF, additional rate cuts are likely to result in further downside. Although multiple rate cuts are likely priced in already. Furthermore, upticks in non-accruals could also result in a sell-off.

For investors looking to buy Bain Capital Specialty Finance, I would keep a close eye on leverage in the coming quarters. Additionally, look to see if deal volumes pick up. Despite spillover, the BDC is still at risk of reducing the dividend, likely sometime in Q1 2026 if we see multiple rate cuts.

Despite their strong fundamentals evidenced by their manageable non-accruals and credit quality, interest rate headwinds, higher leverage, and the potential for further volatility keep me cautious.

data.title: Legence: Little Margin Of Safety Justifies A Hold Rating
data.description:
Investment action

I give a hold rating for Legence Corp. (NASDAQ:LGN). I think that LGN is well-positioned to capture long-term growth from structural themes like AI infrastructure, energy efficiency, and US manufacturing onshoring, but much of this strength already appears priced in. The current valuation reflects very high expectations, leaving little room for error. While the underlying business fundamentals are impressive, the stock’s premium multiple leaves a limited margin of safety in the near term.

Company description

LGN is an end-to-end provider of mission-critical Mechanical, Electrical, and Plumbing (MEP) systems. It covers the entire lifecycle, from engineering and design to fabrication, installation, and long-term maintenance, offering a true one-stop solution. Its core markets include data centers, advanced technology, life sciences, and healthcare. As for its customer base, LGN has a blue-chip client base that includes more than 60% of the Nasdaq-100 (per S-1 filing).

Riding Powerful Secular Tailwinds in High-Growth Markets

What makes LGN's equity story very exciting is its exposure to several powerful, long-duration secular trends. At a glance, LGN may seem like just another player in the infrastructure space, but no, the reality is that LGN rides on strong tailwinds like digitalization, decarbonization, and industrial onshoring. You could say that LGN is a key piece in ensuring these tailwinds materialize (you need someone to build all the data centers and factories).

The most visible tailwind comes from data centers, which represented ~35% of 2024 revenue. This tells us that LGN is a player with experience in dealing with data centers—which are in undersupply today due to lack of power and labor (LGN benefits from the latter)—which gives comfort that LGN has a sizeable portfolio of data center revenue in the pipeline. In the S-1 filing, management cited data from Dodge Construction Network, which projects data center investment to grow at a 22% CAGR through 2029. But the key here is that it’s not only the number of data centers increasing, but also the complexity and value of each project.

AI workloads demand exponentially higher power densities and precision cooling requirements, meaning the mechanical and electrical scope per site is increasing sharply. As we get to the highest and most complex type of data centers—think of the OpenAI Stargate project—it becomes extremely important that the building is built right. Any delay could put off the start date of the data centers, which eats into the operators’ ROI. Hence, I expect LGN to be able to capture an increasing share of demand (vs. subscale and lower-level data center builders) as projects become more technically demanding.

Aside from data centers, LGN is also a clear beneficiary of the US manufacturing re-onshoring wave. To give readers some perspective on how aggressive this wave is, according to data from the S-1 filing, facility investment in advanced industries grew at a staggering 49% CAGR from 2021 to 2024, fueled by federal incentives like the CHIPS Act and IRA. Underlying sentiment also suggests that this momentum will continue for the foreseeable future—while data from one survey (source: S-1 filing) showed 89% of manufacturers are either moving production back to the US or expanding their existing domestic facilities.

These tailwinds should easily support LGN to deliver organic growth ahead, and I would think that its structural exposure to AI infrastructure and domestic manufacturing supports a premium valuation multiple.

Integrated Model and In-House Fabrication Create a Competitive Moat

What differentiates LGN from sub-scale players is the depth of its integration and execution capability. Smaller, sub-scale competitors typically operate in silos. For example, an engineering firm designs the system, a separate contractor handles installation, and another vendor manages maintenance. This fragmented model forces the client to effectively act as their own general contractor, coordinating multiple parties. The result is inefficiency, as designs may not be optimized for installation, which leads to change orders, cost overruns, and timeline delays. Each firm also adds its own profit margin along the way, creating “margin stacking” that inflates the total project cost.

In contrast, LGN operates a fully integrated model that combines engineering & consulting with installation & maintenance, allowing it to deliver end-to-end service. This may not sound like a structural advantage, but it is. My opinion is that by engaging clients early in the project lifecycle, LGN can tap into its engineering expertise to shape design decisions, and that early engagement naturally positions LGN to secure downstream installation and recurring maintenance contracts. Effectively, LGN captures value across the entire lifecycle of a project.

The results speak for themselves. Roughly a quarter of 2024 revenue came from clients utilizing both segments, and that dual-segment customer base has been compounding at an impressive 28% annually since 2021. Also, this integrated approach has driven a 121% net revenue retention rate, reflecting LGN’s ability to transform one-off projects into long-term, recurring revenue streams. For context, this means that LGN can earn 21% more from its existing customer base each year.

The other important piece of LGN’s moat is its in-house fabrication capability, supported by six facilities that bring an industrialized approach to MEP construction. How it works is simple. By shifting complex fabrication off-site into controlled environments, LGN improves precision, shortens project timelines, and significantly reduces cost and execution risks. This not only improves project-level margins but also allows LGN to expand geographically without heavy local facilities. The smaller players simply do not have the scale and balance sheet to support this strategy.

Valuation

The sad part of LGN’s stock (not the business) is that it is not cheap. LGN trades at a whopping 50x forward P/E, way higher than peers like Comfort Systems USA, Inc. (FIX), Stantec Inc. (STN), and WSP Global Inc. (OTCPK:WSPOF), which trade at 32x, 26x, and 26x, respectively. On one end, it makes sense that LGN trades at a premium because of its faster growth outlook (consensus expects high-teens two-year growth CAGR vs. peers with an average of low-teens growth outlook). For LGN, its growth outlook is also backed by a solid $2.8 billion in backlog and awarded contracts, up 29% y/y in Q2 2025—a clear signal that demand remains exceptionally strong.

On the other hand, LGN has much lower margins (10% EBITDA margin vs. peers with low- to mid-teens margins) and also a much higher debt-to-EBITDA ratio (2.8x vs. peers at 0x, 1.9x, and 2.3x, respectively). These justify a discount. So if I were to balance them out, I would think a slight premium makes sense, but not a 22x premium.

At its core, LGN is still a construction company, and that inherently means volatile demand cycles, as customers could delay projects for whatever reasons (financing, strategic decisions, etc.), and that will impact LGN's ability to grow. If it fails to meet expectations, the risk is that the market resets expectations, and LGN sees it collapse to the peers’ level.

Conclusion

I am bullish on LGN’s long-term story. The $2.8 billion backlog and recurring revenue streams provide excellent visibility. That said, with shares trading at ~50x forward earnings—well above peers—I believe risk/reward is balanced at current levels. Any multiple contraction could offset near-term growth. I would look for a more attractive entry point before turning more bullish on the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ubisoft: A Confusing Structure And Fading Turnaround Potential (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Ubisoft (OTCPK:UBSFF) (OTCPK:UBSFY), the stock has fallen another 12%. At the time, the company was already going through a very uncertain period, but with some potential to get things back on track, such as the launch of the new Assassin's Creed and a valuation that was already very depressed, which led me to assign a hold rating.

But since then, the story has become even more confusing. The new structure is convoluted, the company is run through a co-CEO model (who are quite involved with old projects), and it has a history of execution missteps. Ubisoft has become a sell rating in my view.

The Ubisoft Structure: A Mess

The Ubisoft case has already become a turnaround case. The company has some very famous franchises, but it has not executed well.

A completely opposite example would be Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOY) (OTCPK:NTDOF), which has been exploiting the same franchises for decades but does so brilliantly, innovating in its games, releasing movies, and renewing its fan base, in addition to being very efficient in its productions.

Ubisoft certainly failed to do this. To save its franchises and try to see some light at the end of the tunnel, the company carved out its main brands and created a new company dedicated solely to Assassin's Creed, Far Cry, and Rainbow Six, in partnership with Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY), which invested ~€1.1 billion to acquire 25% of this new company.

This structure also comes with a co-CEO model, with Charlie Guillemot and Christophe Derennes, one from the Guillemot family and the other a Ubisoft veteran. Here, there are two points that bother me. The first is that even though it had two options for executives, the company chose two people who are strongly connected to Ubisoft's history, which does not give me the feeling of renewal or a strong change of direction. The second point is the co-CEO model, which, in my view, is extremely challenging, as it can sometimes create conflicts of interest and confusion in the hierarchy. For it to work, the roles need to be very complementary and well defined.

In its blog, Ubisoft mentioned the following about Vantage Studios (this new company):

Vantage Studios represents a first step in Ubisoft's ongoing transformation, with plans to set up additional creative houses and group its brands and franchises under the banner of a shared DNA and development expertise. The goal of Vantage Studios, and future creative houses, is to facilitate stronger and deeper connections between developers and players. This streamlined approach allows for both a higher level of autonomy for developers and a shorter pathway between gathering and implementing player feedback, while still offering the benefit of Ubisoft's expertise, services, tools, and tech.

The story is nice, but I don't find it convincing. Ubisoft needed a revamp, but this doesn't seem to be the kind that benefits shareholders. One factor is that this carve-out distances (dilutes) the “crown jewel” from minority shareholders, since Tencent owns 25% and influences this new division, meaning that most of the value is now in a company that is private, without as much transparency as a listed company.

Similarly, it also creates some potential for misalignments. Tencent obviously wants a return on this division, and Ubisoft also depends heavily on Vantage's results, but at the same time, it will not have complete control over operational decisions, reinvestment, and the like.

What It Needs to Work

The idea isn't bad at all, and depending on the case, I can even see it working. Vantage Studios could deliver higher quality games than it currently does, getting new titles and their monetization right, since it now has both Ubisoft's and Tencent's expertise, which can help not only with technology but also with monetization, access to the Chinese market, and so on.

In this scenario, the life cycle of the main IPs would be longer, and if the financials returned to a healthier level, everyone would win, including minority shareholders.

But the probability of this pie-in-the-sky model working is not that high. It would require a major cultural shift within Ubisoft, perfect alignment between all parties, and flawless execution.

One way I see this happening would be if the company focused on the long term and delivered quality games to its audience through these rich IPs, but again, this does not seem so likely, especially now that the company needs money “at any cost.”

That said, one possible scenario is for this new company to try to squeeze the most out of these new franchises to recover Ubisoft and also make a profit for Tencent. There is nothing that big on the radar, but some of the upcoming games point to a focus on mobile, with a Tom Clancy title and an Assassin's Creed title, as well as a remake of Prince of Persia. In my view, they may work, but they seem to me to be a way of trying to make money faster and possibly with better margins.

The company's great savior, or at least the one that triggers this turning point showing Ubisoft's new phase, is the new Assassin's Creed, which has been called “the most mysterious upcoming Ubisoft game of them all.” To truly win back players and market confidence, Ubisoft will have to innovate in this game, bringing not only technical improvements but also something outside its comfort zone, which makes the outlook even more uncertain.

Ubisoft Stock: Why The Sell Rating

Ubisoft's valuation is obviously quite compressed due to this whole scenario and the uncertainty of recovering its financials. But in my view, it is not compelling enough. Compared to peers such as Take-Two Interactive (TTWO) and Nintendo, or even Square Enix (OTCPK:SQNXF) and Capcom (OTCPK:CCOEY), multiples such as forward EV-to-sales, Ubisoft seems extremely cheap, because while all of them trade at more than 3x, UBSFF's multiple is 1.3x.

But as the turnaround seems far from complete and still shows mixed signals, the only way to envision considerable upside with these multiples would be with some M&A event. Electronic Arts (EA) was sold for $55 billion, or something close to 7x sales for 2026. Of course, the situations are quite different, because EA is a very profitable company, but this shows how the market values this segment, strong franchises, and expertise in game development.

With hundreds of millions of games sold and potential not only to expand into new titles but also into new forms of entertainment (monetizing through series, cartoons, merchandising, etc.), it seems reasonable to say that all of Ubisoft's IPs combined are worth more than €1.3 billion, which is its market cap today.

However, even if we were talking about a sale for twice that amount, the company still has about $1.2 billion in net debt and the risk of burning through part of that cash in the coming quarters in an attempt to turn things around. So it seems to me that the asymmetry is not that positive. It is an upside that can be high, but it is very risky and speculative in nature, while the downside is also high.

The Bottom Line

Despite a compressed valuation, the lack of operational visibility of a turnaround, along with a confusing governance structure and other yellow flags, leads me to downgrade Ubisoft stock to sell.

Even at these prices, I prefer to avoid the company for now and watch from afar for better signs of how management is executing new initiatives.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VGK: A Rising Dollar In H2 Has Not Stopped European Stocks
data.description:
The US Dollar Index (DXY) is quietly staging an impressive second-half recovery in 2025. Compared to a basket of other currencies, the greenback has rallied 2.4% since the start of July. Dollars are now worth more on the global stage than they have been since early August.

Normally, that would be a bearish catalyst for dollar-denominated European stocks. Not so much right now. While the Vanguard FTSE Europe ETF (NYSEARCA:VGK) has not gone parabolic like some U.S. tech stocks, the fund remains in the outperformance zone compared to the S&P 500 year-to-date.

I was bullish on VGK 13 months ago, noting its attractive valuation and mixed chart signals. The fund is up 22% since then, close to the S&P 500’s return. I reiterate a "Buy" rating. The valuation still looks good, while the bulls clearly control the technical situation.

Dollar Up Significantly in 2H2025

Europe and EM remain cheap on a P/E basis (14x-15x), but those multiples have risen from Jan. 1, more than the U.S. P/E has jumped.

Europe continues to trade at a discount to the U.S.—the STOXX 600 is at roughly a 30% discount to the S&P 500, even on a sector‑adjusted basis.

According to the issuer, VGK “seeks to track the performance of the FTSE Developed Europe All Cap Index, which measures the investment return of stocks issued by companies located in the major markets of Europe. It holds shares of companies located in Austria, Belgium, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Greece, Ireland, Italy, the Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, and the United Kingdom.”

VGK is a large ETF, now with more than $35 billion in assets under management as of Oct. 8, 2025. That’s up from $26 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is low at only six basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 2.7%--about double that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been strong for the past six months, earning the product a firm B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The fund scores decently on risk, too, as the standard deviation is in check, while the portfolio is highly diversified.

Finally, on liquidity, VGK is highly tradeable. The 90-day average volume is north of 2.5 million shares, while the median 30-day bid-ask spread is just two basis points, per Vanguard.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar is focused on the large-cap space. Less than 20% of the fund is invested in European SMID caps, and there is a slight bent toward the value style. So, VGK can be a solid diversifier compared to a 100% U.S. allocation.

I like that the price-to-earnings ratio remains low at just 14.8x, which is roughly seven figures below the earnings multiple on the S&P 500. Lastly, the long-term EPS growth rate is solid at 8.6%, resulting in an attractive PEG ratio.

VGK: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

VGK is spread out nicely across the 11 sectors. A plurality of the positions are classified as Financials, which is a cyclical and value area. Information Technology (growth) commands a more than 30% weighting in the SPX, but it’s a mere 7.5% of VGK’s makeup.

Hence, if we see continued alpha from the mega-cap tech niche, then VGK could underperform. VGK could be impacted by the slew of U.S. Financials’ earnings reports due out next week.

VGK: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, October has been a mixed month when scanning the past 10 years of price history data. Better gains have been seen over the November through January stretch. So, being relatively choosy with an entry could be a prudent play—I’ll note where to buy in the next section.

VGK: Bullish November-January Trends

The Technical Take

With a still-solid valuation and robust relative strength in 2025, VGK’s technical situation looks good. Notice in the chart below that the fund is trending higher since the second quarter. A series of higher highs and higher lows asserts that the bulls control the primary trend. Moreover, the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, further buttressing that point.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has been ranging in a constructive zone between 40 and 70. I now see a high amount of volume by price below the current level, which should offer some cushion if we see a year-end retreat. Support is likely to come into play at $76, while further buying pressure may be apparent between $73 and $74. Resistance is now at $82.

VGK: Uptrend Channel, Bullish RSI, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on VGK. I see the Europe ETF as boasting bountiful momentum and an attractive valuation. In the face of a strong dollar lately, VGK has impressively churned higher.

data.title: A Major Market Shift Could Be Coming
data.description:
The S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:SPY) has dramatically outperformed the dividend stock sector ever since the AI boom took off in early 2023. This can be seen by comparing SPY’s performance to that of the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) over that period.

The reason for this is quite simple—leading AI stocks like Meta (META), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Palantir (PLTR) have soared:

The two main reasons for this outperformance are that the AI boom has generated tremendous market optimism over the future profitability of AI, prompting investors to place a premium on leading AI companies, whereas significantly rising interest rates, persistently sticky inflation, and growing uncertainty over the economy and former President Trump’s proposed tariff policies have made the market cautious toward dividend stocks.

However, there are numerous macro indicators suggesting that the S&P 500 is significantly overvalued today and is likely to underperform dividend stocks by a considerable margin moving forward. In this article, I will detail why and share some of my favorite dividend stocks right now.

Overvaluation Red Flags Flashing Across The Market

By virtually any leading market valuation metric, the S&P 500 looks overvalued right now, whether it is the Shiller CAPE sitting at over 40, which is one of the highest readings in decades, or the S&P 500 forward PE ratio of about 23, being far above long-term norms, or even other indicators like the Buffett indicator, the price-to-sales model, or the EBITDA version model. All of those indicators point to the market being strongly overvalued right now. Additionally, this market valuation comes despite the fact that long-term U.S. interest rates (IEF) remain at elevated levels relative to recent history, when typically elevated long-term interest rates mean that discount rates get pushed higher, which pushes equity valuations lower.

Cracks Appearing In The AI Narrative

Additionally, as I recently detailed, AI stocks are facing growing evidence of being in a potential bubble as leading investors have warned that AI stocks are making hundreds of billions of dollars in AI infrastructure investments, despite the actual return on that investment being far from certain. Moreover, early indicators show that companies are having an uneven experience with initial returns on AI projects and the ability to even support the expected compute to make leading megacap tech stocks' AI missions come to fruition are going to be quite daunting to meet thanks to power constraints, technological barriers, and the need to continue raising and deploying immense sums of capital.

Meanwhile, the idea that long-term interest rates are going to fall with the Fed cutting rates is also far from certain, as inflation remains stubbornly high, and the U.S. government deficit is also at bloated levels, Trump's tariffs notwithstanding. This is putting a lot of pressure on the bond market (AGG) because the U.S. government continues to issue an enormous amount of debt, which throws the supply to be a dynamic of bond markets out of whack, thereby pushing long-term interest rates higher. This dynamic is made even worse by the fact that central banks are gracefully moving away from dollars to store value in favor of alternatives like gold (GLD).

Why Dividend Stocks Could Soon Outperform

What this means for dividend stocks is that while they may not get a lot of relief from declining long-term interest rates, they still stand out as a much more attractive alternative to the stock-stopping S&P 500 from a valuation standpoint. With interest rates likely to remain higher for longer on the long end of the curve, and a weakening labor market pointing to a growing risk of recession or, at the very least, short-term economic weakness, investors could very possibly begin to insist more on valuation and profitability on additional capital being deployed since the cost of capital will likely remain elevated. Moreover, economic weakness tends to make investors much more circumspect about where they are deploying capital, as well as how the companies they are investing in are deploying capital.

The Kinds Of Stocks I’m Buying

Therefore, a defensive and somewhat valuation-resistant triple-net lease REIT like W. P. Carey (WPC) or certain infrastructure companies like Enbridge (ENB), or Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC), are much better positioned for a stack-flat-share environment than a high-flying tech stock that is betting heavily on a technology that is likely years away from being able to deliver a satisfactory return on invested capital. Additionally, even the highly profitable mega-cap tech stocks have been priced up to such nosebleed levels that unless they are able to generate very strong growth in the face of a recession and growing questions about returns on investment for their AI push, not to mention being able to successfully secure the power and the compute needed to achieve their AI ambitions, they are likely due for a major correction.

Investor Takeaway

So what does this mean for me? It means that I am steering clear of SPY and the leading megacap tech stocks that are increasingly dominating the index in favor of blue-chip dividend growth stocks. While SCHD is a solid option for long-term investors who do not mind near-term volatility and even periods of sustained underperformance due to its complete exclusion of megacap tech stocks and heavy concentration of energy (XLE), I think investors should be doing even better by picking their own stocks. For me, that includes overweighting, stagflation-resistant opportunities like midstream (AMLP) companies such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) and Energy Transfer (ET) as well as infrastructure and alternative asset management investments like Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC) and Blue Owl Capital (OWL) and even Gold bullion leases as they combine an attractive yield with full ownership and upside potential that comes from precious metals of ownership in gold. I also like a handful of inflation-resistant REITs (VNQ), including Sunbelt multifamily plays like Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) and timberland REITs like Weyerhaeuser (WY). These are the sorts of opportunities I am loading up on at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank of England Set To Cut Rates Further Than Markets Expect
data.description:
By James Smith

Financial markets are giving up on further UK rate cuts...

According to financial markets, the Bank of England’s rate-cutting cycle is virtually over. Investors are pricing little more than one 25 basis-point cut and not until next April. Not for the first time, markets think the UK’s inflation problem is likely to be more enduring than elsewhere.

They aren’t alone. The Bank itself has turned more cautious this summer, as higher food prices have driven headline inflation to almost 4%. The good news is we’re almost certainly close to the peak, but we aren’t likely to see CPI dip much below current levels before year-end. Scarred by the experience of the 2022 energy crisis, the BoE and particularly its Chief Economist, Huw Pill, are worried that we could be on the verge of another persistent bout of above-target inflation.

We think those fears are overblown, not least because, unlike that episode, the jobs market is in a much more fragile state, limiting the ability of workers to shield their disposable incomes through higher pay. Markets, we think, are underpricing the extent of the Bank’s easing cycle that’s still to come. Comments from both hawks and doves at the BoE have emphasised that further cuts are likely.

Markets think UK rates will fall less far than in the US

We now expect the next cut in February

Timing is admittedly less clear. We no longer expect the next cut in November.

But by December, the data should be in a more comfortable position for the Bank. Our forecasts for service sector inflation - the bit of the basket officials are usually most focused on - are roughly half a percentage-point below the BoE’s projections.

We also expect private sector wage growth - currently 4.7% - to fall to within a whisker of 4% in data available before December’s meeting, and below by February’s. Admittedly, this is already reflected in the Bank’s forecasts. But having consistently failed to fall as fast as officials expect, simply seeing wage growth come down in line with expectations would be a major reassurance.

Then there’s the budget, which should add to the case for further easing. Tax hikes are widely expected, yet the details matter. Given the Bank’s extreme focus on headline rates of inflation, officials would likely take a dim view of any measures that add to it, even if, in the longer term, tax hikes tend to alleviate price pressure. The BoE will also be monitoring spending plans and whether next year’s budgets get a top-up. If they don’t, then government expenditure should be much less of a support to the economy in 2026 than it has been in 2025 so far.

Where does that leave our forecasts? We’ve taken out a November cut, but December is possible. However, we narrowly favour February for the next move, on the basis that headline inflation should be a little lower at the start of next year. In total, we expect three rate cuts in 2026.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TeraWulf: Riding The AI Wave
data.description:
As the AI capacity race escalates, TeraWulf Inc. (NASDAQ:WULF) is stepping into the game with massive expansion plans. The company is transitioning from a bitcoin miner to a digital infrastructure provider with major backing from Google (GOOG) (GOOGL). My investment thesis is Bullish on the stock despite the big rally in the last few months.

Digital Infrastructure Transition

Bitcoin miners have an advantage in transitioning to an AI hyperscaler due to their experience working with high-performance computing and access to data center power. TeraWulf recently reported Q2 revenues of $47.6 million due to mining 485 bitcoin at the Lake Mariner Facility with a value of $98,219 per bitcoin.

The company is full-speed ahead building out the digital infrastructure needed to support high-performance computing hosting. TeraWulf went from an under-the-radar bitcoin mining strategy to an HPC platform with a massive deal with Fluidstack to host their AI inference and training business.

TeraWulf now has an expanded deal with Fluidstack for 360 MW (450 MW gross capacity) of critical IT load for a contract valued at $6.7 billion and a potential to reach $16 billion. The contracts are backstopped by a $3.2 billion commitment from Google for a 14% ownership position in TeraWulf to provide the space for a premier AI cloud platform to operate GPU clusters for the likes of Google.

The contracts include $670 million in annual revenue with ~$570 million in net operating income at the site level. As with a lot of other AI infrastructure companies, the business is set up with contracts to immediately report positive cash flows.

The major deal follows the original Core42 lease providing TeraWulf with 2 prime customers now. The Core42 deal had terms amounting to 60 MW of critical IT load at $125 per kilowatt per month, equal to $1.5 million per MW per year, with a 3% annual escalator and a 12-month prepayment.

Management originally targeted an EBITDA margin of ~70%, but the company is now shifting to focus on the NOI metric. The goal is to reach an 85% NOI per site with somewhere around $50 million in annual SG&A expenses currently, which will rise with additional sites.

The bitcoin miner went from a volatile business based on crypto prices to having a massive contracted business. TeraWulf has access to upwards of 750 MW of power at Lake Mariner, almost double the current contract with Fluidstack.

In addition, TeraWulf has the Cayuga site scalable to 400 MW of power via a 3-phase buildout. In total, the company has plans for 1,150 MW of power by 2030.

More Upside

The consensus analyst estimates have revenues doubling each year. TeraWulf is forecasted to report $206 million in 2025 revenues with the numbers jumping to over $820 million in 2027.

The current contracts amount to around $760 million in annual revenues using 522.5 MW of power. TeraWulf has access to enough power to more than double the revenue stream to reach around $1.5 billion in annual revenues for the AI infrastructure business plus whether the company remains in the bitcoin mining business.

Core Scientific, Inc. (CORZ) got a $9 billion offer from CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV). The AI infrastructure company has access to about 1.3 GW of power with ~840 MW of existing HPC gross power for data center capacity while another 500 MW is assigned to mining capacity, leading to revenue targets of $878 million for 2026.

Investors can probably use this deal as an initial target for where TeraWulf could trade over the next year. The stock currently has a market cap of $4.9 billion while Core Scientific is only at $5.3 billion due to the market concerns over the deal closing based on where CoreWeave's stock has traded since announcing the deal.

TeraWulf is looking at adding more facilities, so the upside is definitely in the area where Core Scientific agreed to the deal. The company will raise substantial debt with plans for adding $3 billion in the near term with the additional HPC data center buildout costing up to $10 million per MW of critical IT load, so each 100 MW buildout is up to $1 billion in capex.

The biggest risk to the investment story is for TeraWulf to build out these facilities and acquire additional facilities while AI demand doesn't meet forecasts. Even having Google with a major backstop would lead to a much lower stock price. The company might not be financially ruined with the cash from Google, but the lack of customers and a business model would hit the stock hard.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that TeraWulf investors have already made a lot of money by quickly jumping on this digital infrastructure transition trend early. The stock still appears cheap compared to the opportunity ahead to add additional facilities and build out the current revenue base, especially considering the Core Scientific deal valuation, with lower risk considering Google has already backed $3.2 billion of the Fluidstack revenues.

data.title: VNET Group: Eyes On Latest Order Win And Key Customer's Plans
data.description:
VNET Group (NASDAQ:VNET) is still deserving of being a "Buy"-rated name in my opinion. VNET's orders are picking up again. Its key client has the intent to spend more money in the "Data Center" or "DC" space.

My previous July 28, 2025, write-up touched on its 2Q25 financials preview and the value-unlocking potential of its proposed asset spin.

New Order Bodes Well For VNET's Future

On Sept. 11, VNET issued a media release revealing it "secured a 40 MW wholesale order from a leading internet company for its new Gu'an IDC Campus, strategically located in the Greater Beijing Area."

I think that this development is good for the firm.

VNET's YoY top-line expansion accelerated from +18.3% in Q1 2025 to +22.1% for Apr-Jun 2025. Its actual RMB 2.4 billion sales were equivalent to a 6% beat versus consensus, as per S&P Capital IQ data.

But the group acknowledged in its earnings announcement that "we (only) secured a combined capacity of around 4 MW in retail orders" for 2Q2025. This was worrying, given that orders are a leading indicator of future turnover.

VNET's Historical Wins (Megawatt Terms)

UBS published a report (not publicly available) titled "Chinese Internet Data Centre Sector" on Sept. 19, 2025. The chart (source: UBS) above indicates that the company's "order drought" is finally over in Q3 with the fresh 40 MW addition to the pipeline.

Also, VNET's partners are no longer that reliant on Nvidia's (NVDA) chip supply, as feared earlier.

The firm's press release from the prior month mentioned it "will provide reliable, customized services that enable the (Chinese) customer (40 MW order) to deploy domestic chips (author emphasis) for its core business."

This is consistent with SA News' end-Aug piece noting that "DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) is using some of Huawei's processors to train smaller artificial intelligence models" to "reduce its reliance on Nvidia."

The enterprise's near-term outlook is promising.

VNET took part in Goldman Sachs' (GS) investor event last month. Its disclosures were detailed in a Sept. 5 research note (inaccessible in public) titled "VNET Group: Asia Leaders Conference." The company noted then that its "3Q25 order volume" could "hopefully approach the 1Q25 level." It has observed "chips" from non-NVDA players like "Huawei, Moore Threads, MetaX, and Iluvatar are becoming mainstream."

I see the firm winning more orders in the remainder of this year, considering China's chip supply is more diversified now.

Its Client Has Huge Ambitions

Alibaba (BABA) is "committed to increasing its global data center capacity by 10x by 2032," according to Citigroup's (C) research citing the former's comments at the late-Sep "Apsara Conference."

This has substantial implications for VNET's growth runway in the long run.

The total maximum power output of BABA's DCs was around 1.35 GW three years ago. Its '32 goal translates into an additional 1,220 MW ((13500-1350)/10) of new DC capacity every year going forward. This figure has yet to consider demand coming from other tech behemoths in China, such as Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) (OTCPK:TCTZF) and Huawei. Research firm "Network Installers" is forecasting annual increases of +3.6 GW for the entire Chinese DC sector during the 2025-2030 period.

As a comparison, VNET has won less than 0.2 GW of new orders in 2025 thus far. It is likely to grow at a faster pace in the years ahead if Alibaba lives up to her promise.

Separately, the company's intermediate-term performance might also surprise on the upside.

BABA has also guided that it will "boost its capital spending on AI infrastructure beyond the originally planned 380 billion yuan ($53 billion) over the next three years (2025-2027)," according to SA News.

The sell-side anticipates that VNET's EBITDA will rise by +28% and +29% (source: S&P Capital IQ) in FY 2026 and FY 2027, respectively. This is a big improvement compared to its actual 19% EBITDA increase last year.

Its customer Alibaba is projecting higher-than-expected cloud capex, which provides support for the analysts' optimism surrounding VNET.

Comparable Transaction Suggests That VNET Is Undervalued

Bain Capital revealed last month that "its data center portfolio company WinTrix DC Group" is divesting "100% of its equity interest in its China operations (Chindata)" for "US$4 billion." This sales consideration is equivalent to a "price-to-book" or "P/B" of 3x.

VNET's forward FY 2026 P/B is 2.5x as per S&P Capital IQ, implying a possible capital appreciation of at least +20% (3/2.5-1).

Chindata used to be listed on NASDAQ before Bain's 2023 take-private offer. Its guided FY 2023 revenues were around RMB 6B prior to de-listing, or 20% below what VNET actually achieved that year.

It's fair to say that VNET deserves to trade at a P/B of above 3x, considering a size-related valuation premium.

You Should Think About These Risk Factors

Slower-than-anticipated order momentum will limit VNET's re-rating potential. That can happen if Chinese chips fail to be a competent substitute for NVDA's H20 or B30A.

BABA's actual cloud-related capital expenditures in the future could fall short of expectations. It's normal for companies to reassess their capital allocation priorities as the external environment changes.

My Closing Remarks

I believe that VNET continues to be an appealing investment.

There are favorable read-throughs from its own order wins and its customer's capex guide. The stock is also trading below fair value, taking reference from a private equity firm's recent asset monetization deal.

data.title: Asia's Export Pulse Weakens, Policy Support Persists
data.description:
By Deepali Bhargava

Export headwinds are beginning to emerge

After Asia’s GDP growth surprised to the upside in the first half of the year, signs of softening export momentum are beginning to emerge. Exports to the US fell 8% YoY in August, reversing the 3% YoY growth seen cumulatively from April to July. In contrast, exports to the rest of the world (excluding the US) rose a robust 9% YoY in August and 10% YoY over the prior four months. The diversification away from the US is primarily driven by intra-Asia trade and Europe. Overall, we expect slower Asia export growth in the near term as global demand slows down.

Robust consumption and capex are offsetting external headwinds in key Asian markets

Domestic consumption is helping offset some of the drag from weaker export growth, particularly in demand-driven economies such as India and the Philippines, as well as in Australia and Malaysia, where resilient labour markets continue to support spending. Fiscal measures are also contributing to consumption growth in several economies, including India, Korea, and Indonesia, with rising prospects of additional stimulus in Japan and China.

In a bold move to stimulate domestic demand and promote tax equity, India has overhauled its Goods and Services Tax (GST) structure. Taxes on essential items, such as food products, basic household goods, consumer electronics, and small motor vehicles, have been significantly reduced. Meanwhile, life-saving drugs and health and life insurance have been made entirely tax-free. These reforms are designed to directly boost consumption, particularly among middle and lower-income households.

Investment growth has remained resilient in several Asian economies. Taiwan and Malaysia stand out, likely benefiting from continued capital expenditure in the tech sector. However, business sentiment around future investment is turning cautious, as reflected in recent surveys, amid signs of a slowdown in global trade.

Subdued inflation across Asia, with Australia as an outlier

Inflation remains subdued across most of Asia, driven by overcapacity in several sectors in China and easing food and fuel price pressures. While recent typhoons may temporarily lift food prices, improved rainfall conditions expected in the fourth quarter should help mitigate the impact. Softer global oil price inflation in 2026 is also likely to support a benign inflation outlook.

Australia stands out as an exception. The August CPI spike to 3% YoY raises questions about whether this is a temporary blip or the start of more persistent inflationary pressures. Notably, housing-related components, such as rents and the cost of new dwellings, showed renewed strength, suggesting the sector may already be responding to earlier rate cuts. Additionally, services prices, including holidays, travel, and insurance, rose sharply (1.5-3% QoQ), pointing to a rebound in consumption demand. On the growth front, stronger-than-expected GDP in the first half of the year was driven by robust consumer spending. As a result, the likelihood of a rate cut at the November meeting has meaningfully diminished.

Monetary policy outlook: easing bias persists

Overall, we expect most Asian economies under our coverage, except Japan, to remain on track to ease monetary policy further, particularly as real interest rates remain elevated, especially in Southeast Asia.

Content Disclaimer

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Persists
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, neutral sentiment increased, and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 3.0 percentage points to 45.9%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the fourth consecutive week. Optimism was last higher on December 5, 2024 (48.3%).

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 0.6 percentage points to 18.5%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 64th time in 66 weeks. This is the first time neutral sentiment has been below 20% for four consecutive weeks since 2007.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 3.5 percentage points to 35.6%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 45th time in 47 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 6.5 percentage points to 10.2%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the first time in 10 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members which market-capitalization style of stocks they expect to outperform over the next six months.

Here is how they responded:

  • Small-cap stocks: 33.5%.
  • Large-cap stocks: 29.2%.
  • Mid-cap stocks: 17.3%.
  • Not sure/no opinion: 20.0%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 45.9%, up 3.0 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 18.5%, up 0.6 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 35.6%, down 3.5 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.
